---
title: "Configuration files"
description: "Configure `check-datapackage` settings using a configuration file."
order: 5
---

`check-datapackage` can be configured through command-line options and a
configuration file. This guide covers configuration files for the CLI.
For command-line usage, see the [CLI guide](/docs/guide/cli.qmd). For
the Python `Config` class used with `check()`, see
[Configuring the checks](/docs/guide/config.qmd).

A configuration file lets you set preferences once, so you don't have to
repeat them every time you run `check-datapackage check`.
`check-datapackage` supports two configuration files: `.cdp.toml`
`pyproject.toml`. These files should be placed in the root of your
project---`check-datapackage` will find them automatically.

Use `.cdp.toml` if you want a file dedicated to `check-datapackage`
settings, or `pyproject.toml` if your Data Package is a Python project
that already uses `pyproject.toml` and you prefer to keep all config
settings in one place.

::: callout-note
Settings provided via the CLI or Python interface always take priority
over the configuration file.
:::

## Configurable settings

Before diving into editing your configuration, let’s see which settings
can be configured via the configuration file:

- `strict`: If `true`, include checks for Data Package properties marked
  as "SHOULD" in addition to those marked as "MUST". Defaults to
  `false`.
- `exclusions`: Exclude issues by
  [JSONPath](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9535.html), issue type,
  or both.

Note that the positional `source` argument cannot be set via the
configuration file; it must be passed via the command line or Python
interface.

## Configuration file syntax

Both configuration files use TOML syntax. The examples below start with
`.cdp.toml`, then show the `pyproject.toml` variant. All the examples
below use [Soil's](https://soil.seedcase-project.org) built-in
[`simple` Data Package](https://soil.seedcase-project.org/docs/reference/example):

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
simple_example="$(python -c 'from seedcase_soil import Example; print(Example.simple.path)')"
```

### Strict checking

Before creating a configuration file, we can see that the simple example
data package passes all checks:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check "$simple_example"
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
import seedcase_soil as so

so.format_output_for_docs(["cdp", "check", str(so.Example.simple.path)])
```

Now, create a `.cdp.toml` file and enable strict checking:

``` {.toml filename=".cdp.toml"}
strict = true
```

Now, when you run `check-datapackage check`, strict mode is enabled and
the example package does not pass all the stricter checks:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check "$simple_example"
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pathlib import Path

config_path = Path(".cdp.toml")
config_path.write_text("""
strict = true
""")

try:
    so.format_output_for_docs(["cdp", "check", str(so.Example.simple.path)])
finally:
    config_path.unlink(missing_ok=True)
```

This is the same as if we would have run
`check-datapackage check --strict "$simple_example"` before creating the
configuration file.

If you prefer to configure `check-datapackage` via `pyproject.toml`, the
syntax is almost the same as above; the only difference is that you need
to include a heading to indicate where in the file `check-datapackage`
can find the settings. Open up your `pyproject.toml` (or create one in
the root of your project) and add a new table header
[tool.check-datapackage]. Then put the same configuration settings as
you used above in `.cdp.toml` below this table header.

``` {.toml filename="pyproject.toml"}
[tool.check-datapackage]
strict = true
```

### Exclusions

Above, we saw above that the simple example data package fails strict
checking because the required `id` property is missing. To exclude only
that issue, add an exclusion to the `.cdp.toml` file that matches both
the JSONPath and issue type:

``` {.toml filename=".cdp.toml"}
strict = true

[[exclusions]]
jsonpath = "$.id"
type = "required"
```

Running the same command now passes:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check "$simple_example"
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
config_path.write_text("""
strict = true
[[exclusions]]
jsonpath = "$.id"
type = "required"
""")

try:
    so.format_output_for_docs(["cdp", "check", str(so.Example.simple.path)])
finally:
    config_path.unlink(missing_ok=True)
```

If you want to add different exclusions, you can keep adding more
`[[exclusions]]` below this one.

### Extensions

You can also add required checks in your configuration file. For
example, to require the `id` property, add a
`[[extensions.required_checks]]` table:

``` {.toml filename=".cdp.toml"}
[[extensions.required_checks]]
jsonpath = "$.id"
message = "The Data Package must include an id."
```

Run the check:

``` {.bash filename="Terminal"}
check-datapackage check "$simple_example"
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
config_path.write_text("""
[[extensions.required_checks]]
jsonpath = "$.id"
message = "The Data Package must include an id."
""")

try:
    so.format_output_for_docs(["cdp", "check", str(so.Example.simple.path)])
finally:
    config_path.unlink(missing_ok=True)
```

If you use `pyproject.toml`, put the same nested table under
`[tool.check-datapackage]`:

``` {.toml filename="pyproject.toml"}
[tool.check-datapackage]

[[tool.check-datapackage.extensions.required_checks]]
jsonpath = "$.id"
message = "The Data Package must include an id."
```

You can add more required checks by adding more
`[[extensions.required_checks]]` tables. Custom checks require a Python
function for their `check` argument, so define
[`CustomCheck`](/docs/reference/CustomCheck.qmd) extensions in Python
rather than in a TOML configuration file.
